glyphs.label: NAT TYPES IN PALO ALTO - PRACTICAL USE CASES

What it does:
Many private IPs -> one public IP,
differentiated by port numbers.

When used:
- Standard internet access for office users
- One ISP public IP for many employees
- Cloud VMs in a private VNet needing outbound access

What it does:
Private IPs -> pool of public IPs,
one-to-one per session (no port overload).

When used:
- Applications that break with PAT
- Rotate a public /29 block across sessions
- Each session must have a unique public IP

Example
50 servers translated to one of
203.0.113.10-203.0.113.20 dynamically per session

What it does:
One specific private IP -> always the same
public IP (bidirectional by default).

When used:
- Server must always appear as one public IP
- API whitelisting or partner firewalls
- Server also receives inbound traffic

Example
Internal mail server 10.0.0.50 always egresses as
203.0.113.30, and partners whitelist that IP

What it does:
Inbound traffic to a public IP is translated
to a fixed internal private IP, all ports.

When used:
- Publishing a server fully to the Internet
- 1:1 mapping for all traffic to one host
- Often paired with source NAT for return traffic

Example
Anyone hitting 203.0.113.30 on any port
gets forwarded to 10.0.0.50

What it does:
Translates destination IP and port to an internal server.

When used:
- One public IP, multiple internal servers
- RDP 3389, HTTPS 443, SSH 22 to different hosts
- Expose only specific ports
Example: one public IP (203.0.113.5) publishing multiple servers

What it does:
Redirects traffic hitting one port to a different port on the same or different host.

When used:
- Internal clients using a public IP/FQDN (U-turn NAT)
- Change service ports (8000 -> 80)
- Load balancer redirection
- Hide internal port numbers

One public IP, many users              -> PAT / DIPP
Pool of IPs, each session gets one      -> DYNAMIC NAT
Fixed public IP always needed           -> STATIC SNAT

Expose whole server (all ports)        -> STATIC DNAT
One IP publishing many services         -> PORT FORWARDING
Internal users use public name/IP        -> PORT REDIRECTION
NAT POLICY AND SECURITY POLICY ARE SEPARATE

NAT POLICY
(Translate Address)

- Translates source/destination IP and port
- Happens first in the flow
- Defines how the address changes

SECURITY POLICY
(Allow or Deny Traffic)

- Matches post-NAT destination zone
- Decides whether traffic is allowed
- Logs, inspects, and applies rules

1. NAT happens on the firewall - both inbound and outbound.

2. Create the correct security policy after NAT is in place.

3. NAT rule order matters: top to bottom, first match wins.

4. Use hit counts and logs to verify NAT and security policies.
